title: Kundalini and the Immune System: Why You Get Sick During Awakening
meta_description: You're curled up, shaking, feverish, again. It feels like the flu, but it's not the flu. It's something else, something deeper, something that rips through
body:
Kundalini and the Immune System: Why You Get Sick During Awakening

You're curled up, shaking, feverish, again. It feels like the flu, but it's not the flu. It's something else, something deeper, something that rips through you and leaves you wondering if you're dying. You've been feeling this way a lot lately, ever since… well, ever since things started getting weird. Ever since the electricity started running through your spine, ever since the visions, the intense emotions, the feeling like your whole damn world is being torn apart and rebuilt, piece by excruciating piece. You're scared, I know. You're alone in the dark, and your body feels like it's betraying you. I've been there. I know that particular brand of hell. Let's talk about it. Let's talk about why you're getting sick during what they call "spiritual awakening."

First off, let's get one thing straight: you're not imagining this. You're not making it up. The physical symptoms are real. The exhaustion, the aches, the sudden fevers, the unexplained rashes, the weird digestive issues - they're all part of the package for a lot of us. And it's terrifying because doctors often can't find anything "wrong" with you, right? They run tests, scratch their heads, and maybe suggest it's stress or anxiety. And yeah, it is stress, but it's a specific kind of stress, a cosmic stress on your system. It's your body trying to keep up with an internal explosion. Your immune system? It's on the front lines of that war.

The Body's Internal Housekeeping

Think of kundalini awakening - or whatever you want to call this chaotic process - as a massive, unsolicited renovation project happening inside you. Imagine someone comes into your house, knocks down walls, rips out old plumbing, rewires the entire electrical system, and then expects you to live in it while they're doing it. That's your body right now. It's messy. It's dusty. There are exposed wires everywhere. And your immune system? Its job is to clean up the debris, fight off any "invaders" that might take advantage of the chaos, and try to restore some semblance of order.

When you're going through this, your nervous system is in overdrive. It's constantly firing, trying to process the sheer volume of energy and information. This isn't just mental or emotional; it's deeply physical. Your cells are literally vibrating at a different frequency. Old cellular memories, traumas stored in your tissues, they're all being stirred up and released. It's like your body is doing a deep clean, a detox on every level. And what happens when you detox? You feel like crap, right? You get headaches, you get tired, you get irritable. Now multiply that by a thousand, and you're getting close to what kundalini does.

Your immune system perceives all this internal upheaval as a threat. It doesn't distinguish between a foreign virus and a massive energy surge ripping through your chakras, dissolving old energetic blockages. It just knows there's a huge internal stressor. So, it ramps up. It releases inflammatory markers. It sends out white blood cells. It tries to contain the "damage." This heightened state of alert can manifest as chronic low-grade inflammation, aches and pains that move around your body, or even sudden, intense flu-like symptoms as your body tries to purge what it perceives as toxins. It's not necessarily a cold or flu in the traditional sense, but your body's immune response mimics it because it's trying to get rid of something that doesn't belong - in this case, old energy, old patterns, old trauma.

The Stress Response: Fight, Flight, or Freeze, Permanently On

You're living in a constant state of fight-or-flight, aren't you? That feeling of anxiety, of being wired but tired, of your heart racing for no reason. That's your sympathetic nervous system stuck in the "on" position. This isn't just an emotional state; it has deep physical consequences. When your body is constantly stressed, it diverts resources away from things like digestion, repair, and immune function, because it thinks you're about to be eaten by a saber-toothed tiger. It's prioritizing immediate survival. But the problem is, the tiger isn't external; it's internal. And it's not going away.

Chronic stress suppresses your immune system's ability to fight off actual infections. So, you might find you're catching every bug going around, or old dormant viruses (like Epstein-Barr or shingles) suddenly reactivate. Your body's reserves are depleted. It's trying to rebuild your entire energetic structure while simultaneously trying to fight off what it perceives as an internal invasion, all while you're still trying to function in the world. It's an impossible task, and something has to give. Often, that "something" is your physical health. You might experience:

  • Frequent colds or flu-like symptoms
  • Unexplained rashes or skin irritations
  • Digestive issues: IBS, nausea, changes in appetite
  • Chronic fatigue or exhaustion that sleep doesn't touch
  • Muscle aches, joint pain, or fibromyalgia-like symptoms
  • Headaches or migraines
  • Sensitivity to light, sound, or chemicals

Does that sound familiar? It should, because it's a common list for those of us who've been through this. It's not fun. It's terrifying. You feel like you're losing your mind and your body at the same time. You might feel like you're dying, honestly. I know I did. There were nights I truly thought I wouldn't wake up.

The Unpacking of Trauma and the Immune System's Response

Here's the thing: kundalini doesn't just awaken spiritual awareness; it also cleans house. And that house includes all the stored trauma, all the unresolved emotions, all the suppressed memories that are literally held in your body. It's not just "in your head." Your cells remember. Your fascia remembers. Your nervous system remembers. And when this energy starts moving, it starts dislodging all that old stuff. It's like shaking up a dusty old rug - all the dirt and debris that was settled is now flying around. And your immune system is there, trying to deal with it.

When old traumas surface, your body can react as if they're happening all over again. This can trigger a massive inflammatory response. It's your body's way of trying to heal the old wounds, but it can be incredibly disruptive. You might find yourself experiencing physical symptoms that mirror the emotional pain you're processing. For example, if you had a lot of grief stored, you might get chest pains or lung issues. If you had unexpressed anger, you might get liver issues or skin eruptions. It's not a direct one-to-one, but there's a strong correlation for many people. Your immune system is effectively clearing out old energetic waste, and that process can make you feel incredibly unwell.

You need to understand that this isn't just "in your head." Your physical body is undergoing a literal transformation. Your DNA is being activated, your neural pathways are being rewired, your endocrine system is going haywire. This isn't a gentle process. It's a forceful one. And it demands a tremendous amount of energy from your body, leaving your immune system vulnerable and often overworked. If you want to understand more about the physical manifestations, I found The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) to be really validating. It shows you're not alone in these bizarre symptoms.

What You Can Do: Support, Not Fight

So, what do you do when your body feels like it's falling apart and your immune system is either overreacting or underperforming? You support it. You don't fight it. You don't try to suppress the symptoms with quick fixes, because those symptoms are often your body's way of trying to heal. Instead, you create the conditions for your body to do its job with as much ease as possible.

Nourishment and Rest

This sounds basic, but it's crucial. Your body is burning through nutrients and energy at an alarming rate. You need to feed it good, clean food. I'm talking whole foods, lots of vegetables, healthy fats, good protein. Cut out the junk, the sugar, the processed crap that just adds to the inflammatory burden. And for god's sake, rest. I know it's hard when your nervous system is buzzing, but you need to prioritize sleep, naps, quiet time. Your body does its major repair work when you're at rest. If you're constantly pushing, you're just digging yourself into a deeper hole. You're not lazy; you're undergoing a massive internal process. Respect that.

Hydration is also key. Drink plenty of clean water. Your body is flushing out a lot of old stuff, and it needs water to do that efficiently. Think of it as helping the plumbing system during that house renovation I mentioned earlier. You need water to carry away the debris.

Gentle Movement and Grounding

You might not feel like doing a high-intensity workout, and that's okay. In fact, it's probably not what your body needs right now. Instead, focus on gentle movement: walking in nature, slow stretching, gentle yoga, tai chi. These help move energy without overwhelming your system. They also help ground you, which is incredibly important when you feel like you're floating outside your body. Spend time in nature. Put your bare feet on the earth. Connect with something stable and real. This helps calm your nervous system and signals to your immune system that you're not in immediate danger.

I found a lot of solace in books that explained what the hell was happening to me. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) were lifesavers. They helped me understand I wasn't going crazy and that these physical symptoms were part of a recognized process. Knowing that alone can take some of the fear away, you know?

Emotional Release and Self-Compassion

As I said, this process brings up a lot of old emotional gunk. Don't suppress it. Find healthy ways to release it: crying, journaling, talking to a trusted friend or therapist, screaming into a pillow. Allowing these emotions to move through you prevents them from getting stuck in your body and creating more physical symptoms. Your immune system is already busy; don't give it more work by bottling up your feelings.

And for the love of everything, be kind to yourself. This isn't a race. There's no "right" way to go through this. You're doing something incredibly difficult, something most people will never experience. There will be days you feel like you're failing, days you just want it all to stop. That's okay. Allow yourself to be where you are. Give yourself permission to rest, to cry, to not be productive. Your worth isn't tied to how much you can do right now. Your worth is inherent. This process is forcing you to learn that, often the hard way.

Look, I'm not going to sugarcoat it. It's a rough ride. It's often isolating and terrifying. But you're not alone. What you're experiencing is real, and your body's reactions, including the illness, are a natural part of this intense purification and restructuring. Your immune system isn't failing you; it's working overtime to keep you alive and integrate these massive changes. Support it, listen to it, and be patient. This too shall pass. Or rather, you'll pass through it, changed, often stronger, eventually healthier, but definitely not the same.

If you're feeling completely overwhelmed and need to understand the wider context of these experiences, I highly recommend The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link). It really puts things into perspective and helps you see that what's happening isn't just you; it's a known, albeit rarely discussed, human experience. You're going to get through this. Just breathe, and keep going.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
image_alt: Kundalini and the Immune System: Why You Get Sick During Awakening
